observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Management agreed to direct the Municipal
Accountant to use the appropriate accounts to record transfer of assets
to General Fund from Trust Fund and prepare the necessary adjusting
entries as follows:
Subsidy From Other Funds 7,311,023.64
Subsidy To Other Funds 7,311,023.64
Transfer from GF of Unspent DRRM 7,311,023.64
Prior Period Adjustments 7,311,023.64

We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to
ensure correct recording of transactions in the ensuing years for the fair
presentation of accounts.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal
Treasurer agreed to timely reconcile records per cashbook as well as the
general and subsidiary ledgers before the latter foot and close the books
at the end of each month.

We also recommended and the two officers agreed to conduct regular
reconciliation of records at least every end of each quarter and any
adjustment must be corrected and resolved promptly.

We recommended and the Management agreed to call the attention of
the employees who are still in service, to fully settle the amount advanced
as soon as possible. Otherwise, withhold their salaries until the cash
advances are fully settled.

Furthermore, we recommended and the Management agreed to request
for the write-off of long outstanding and dormant receivables.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure
that no additional cash advance be granted to any official or employee
with pending unliquidated cash advance.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to complete the
physical inventory taking, render a report thereon and update the
Property Records based on the result of the inventory and reconcile with
the accounting records.

Henceforth, periodic inventory taking and reconciliation of property and
accounting records must be regularly undertaken and regarded with
utmost importance.

We recommended and the management agreed to strictly adhere to the
provisions of Republic Act No. 9184 and COA Circular No. 2009-001 by
ensuring that Purchase Requests are prepared, and Purchase Orders are
issued before the actual consumption or delivery of goods and services.

We further recommended and the management agreed to ensure that all
procurement transactions are properly documented with accurate dates
reflecting the actual sequence of events.

Lastly, we recommended and the management agreed to direct the end-
user or the BAC to submit the Purchase Orders within five (5) working
days from issuance thereof.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to submit the
supporting documents required.

Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant agreed to only process and pay
disbursements with complete documentation in consonance with existing
rules and regulations.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare and
immediately submit the monthly bank reconciliation statements of all
depository accounts for CY 2024 pursuant to existing regulations and
validate the existence of cash in bank balances as presented in the
financial statements.

We recommended and the Management agreed to require the Municipal
Treasurer and Municipal Accountant to exert extra effort in preparing
and submitting on time the Municipality’s accounting records, financial
reports and statements.

We also recommended and the Management agreed that proper
monitoring be made so that these records and reports are submitted
promptly in accordance with the salient provisions of PD No. 1445, COA
Circular No. 2009-006 and the Manual on the NGAS for LGUs, Volume
I.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare and
maintain complete subsidiary ledgers and submit promptly in succeeding
years the complete set of journals and ledgers in printed and electronic
copies to the Office of the Auditor.

3. We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the General Services Officer to prepare the Property Cards and other necessary documents, and to maintain a separate record to aid the preparation of the Report of Physical Count of PPE every end of the year.
4. We further recommend that the Municipal Accountant fill out the PPELC completely and conduct the necessary reconciliations once the RPCPPE is available.

5. We further recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the issuance of ARE for all procured or acquired items which shall be used to report on the receipt of property and equipment for official use.

9. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Assessor coordinate and exert collaborative efforts in reconciling the market value of Land as required under existing rules and regulations.

10. We further recommend that the Municipal Accountant restate the value of the Land to its revalued amounts, and recognize any revaluation increases and/or decreases in the books once the appraisal/revaluation is done.

11. We recommend that the Management facilitate the issuance of the appropriate Executive Order to establish the Municipality’s GAD M&E Team to formalize the monitoring of GAD related activities and ensure its success pursuant to PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01.

12. We recommend that the Municipal Accountant in coordination with the Acting Municipal Treasurer return the unspent balances of fund transfers to the Source Agencies after confirming that the purpose of the fund transfer agreed in the MOA has already been completed.

13. We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant regularly monitor the receipts and utilization of fund transfers and return any unspent amount to the Source Agencies after the the implementation depending on condition in the MOA.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that concerned officials ensure that all properties of the Municipality are insured with the General Insurance Fund of the GSIS to protect the same against loss or damage and in case of loss or destruction, the Municipality can at least recover the costs of these assets.

observations.recommendation: We recommend compliance of the following: Create a Committee for the inspection and appraisal of the unserviceable properties and prepare report. observations.recommendation:
We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Assessor agreed to coordinate and exert collaborative efforts in reconciling the market value of Land as required under existing rules and regulations.
We further recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to restate the value of the Land to its revalued amounts, and recognize any revaluation increases and/or decreases in the books once the appraisal/revaluation is done.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant and Acting Municipal Treasurer agreed to timely reconcile records per cashbook as well as the general and subsidiary ledgers before the latter foot and close the books at the end of each month.
We also recommended and the two offices agreed to conduct regular reconciliation of records at least every end of each quarter and any adjustment must be corrected and resolved promptly.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to make a detailed review of the accounts and determine the cause/s of the unreconciled amounts. Thereafter, make the necessary adjustments/corrections in the books of accounts to present the correct balance of the affected accounts. Henceforth, exercise care in recording and classifying the Municipality’s financial transactions to ensure that the balances of the reciprocal accounts are always the same.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct the LDRRMO and the Municipal Accountant to regularly prepare a monthly Report on the Utilization of the LDRRMF and submit the same to the Audit Team within the period prescribed pursuant to COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to intensify the real property tax collection to meet the targets so that the Municipality can provide additional funds to finance infrastructure and basic services to its constituents.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to send a written notice to the payees for the cancellation of the checks and require them to surrender the checks for issuance of a new one. Furthermore, we recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to prepare a Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to record the adjustment of stale checks in the books if the checks are still in the Office of the Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended and the Municipal Treasurer agreed to release the share of real property tax and other taxes due the barangays as mandated by the Local Government Code in order to augment the barangays’ meager income and to finance developmental programs and projects for the benefit of their constituents. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to reconcile her records with the Municipal Treasurer on unremitted barangay RPT shares as soon as the records are available.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee stop splitting government contracts, ensure that the procurement activities are specified in the APP, and that the procurement of goods and projects are undertaken based on the approved schedule.

We recommended and the MSWD Officer agreed to ensure the preparation and submission of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed and Balances to appropriate offices, copy furnish the COA Auditor, within ten (10) days after the end of the quarter to enable proper recording, monitoring and evaluation of donations received by the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require the Municipal Health Officer to submit immediately the required supporting documents to establish the validity of SRA payment. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure strict compliance with the prescribed guidelines before granting any allowances to personnel.

We recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to require the Municipal Health Officer to submit immediately the required supporting documents to establish the validity of AHDP payment. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure strict compliance with the prescribed guidelines before granting any allowances to personnel.
3. We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to reconcile the difference between the actual amounts presented in the SCBAA and the amount in the SFPer.

4. We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations that the LCE directs the City Accountant to revisit the provisions of the IPSAS and other COA issuances particularly on the preparation and presentation of accounts in the FS.

5. We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to undertake a detailed reconciliation/analysis of the accounts and investigate causes of the error/discrepancies for the purpose of booking up the necessary adjustments to arrive at the correct balances of the negative accounts.

6. We reiterated our previous years’ audit recommendation that the City Accountant require all the depository banks to strictly comply with the submission of the mandated BS and supporting documents for all depository accounts maintained by the City Government within five days after the end of each month. In turn, within ten days from the receipt of such BS, the same should be reconciled with the General Ledgers which shall be the basis for preparation of BRS; and henceforth, the BRS must be regularly submitted to the Auditor within ten days after the end of each month together with the paid checks and debit/credit memos. Likewise, prepare the necessary journal entry vouchers to record the reconciling items affecting the accounts.

7. We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the Management direct the City Accountant to reconcile with the City Treasurer the Cash Accounts records on a quarterly basis to reflect an accurate and reliable balance at any particular period.

11. Apply requests for write-off of dormant account to the ATL and SA/ RD/ AC, whichever is applicable, on a quarterly basis. This shall help in reducing the balances of unliquidated cash advances presented in the books.
12. Exert all possible efforts to reconcile the balances presented in the books and schedule of cash advances to produce a more accurate presentation of balances.
13. For Trust Fund unliquidated accounts, exert and effort to confirm with the respective source agencies whether they still have outstanding unliquidated fund transfers.
14. Direct the CAO to maintain and prepare PPELC with the required format as shown in Annex 9 on the MNGAS for LGUs and update the same.

15. The City Accountant and Other Officers concerned should provide for the depreciation on the depreciable assets. The Property Supply Officer should ensure that the Property Custodian keeps and maintains updated Property Card for each PPE account to readily provide pertinent data, thus facilitate the preparation of the Schedule of Depreciation which shall be the basis of recording in the books of accounts the appropriate depreciation expenses pertaining to the current year which should be charged against its appropriate depreciation expense accounts. These are necessary in order to present fairly the PPE accounts with its corresponding Accumulated Depreciation Expense Accounts in the FS.

19. Instruct the City General Services Officer and the City Accountant to maintain updated Stock Cards and SLC and regularly reconcile records to detect discrepancies and effect immediate corrections.
20. Establish relevant inventory account cited above

21. We reiterated our prior years audit recommendation to provide for Allowance for Impairment Accounts in the books in order to present fairly the net realizable value of the Receivable Accounts in the financial statements.
24. The City Accountant to carefully monitor the balances per ledgers, schedules and reports and reconcile the balance of the Trust Liability-DRRM with the Cash-in Bank; and

25. City Budget Officer to coordinate with the Local Finance Committee in the monitoring and reconciliation of LDRRM budget, cash availability and financial statement account balances to determine amount still not fully utilized after five years that should be reverted back to the general fund.

26. The City Budget Officer to review the budget of the different Departments/Office as to the correct expense classification and include on Other MOE only those which cannot be classified under any specific expense titles pursuant to COA Circular No 2015-009. In case the Other MOE account is used, specify the items of expenditures to be charged to the said account; and

27. The City Accountant to record all the transactions of the City using the proper accounts prescribe in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs so that transactions are faithfully and correctly presented in the financial statements to provide reliable data to the users thereof.

28. We recommended that the City Accountant be directed to properly review the COA chart of accounts and properly classify future transactions as to their proper accounts. Further, the Office of the Team Leader is always open for consultation as to the proper recording of unique transactions.
32. We recommended that the City Assessor to regularly conduct general revision of assessment of real property every three (3) years in accordance with the existing laws, rules and regulations.

1.16 We reiterated our previous years’ audit recommendation that the City
Accountant:
a. require all the depository banks to strictly comply with the
submission of the BS including DM/CM, paid checks, etc. for all
depository accounts maintained by the City Government within five
days after the end of each month;
b. reconcile the BS with the General Ledgers, within 10 days from the
receipt of such BS, and prepare BRS;
c. investigate reasons of discrepancies and prepare the necessary
journal entry vouchers to record the reconciling items affecting the
accounts; and
d. make an effort to reconcile with the City Treasurer the Cash
Accounts records, on a quarterly basis.

2.19 We reiterated the following recommendations:
a. Apply requests for write-off of dormant account to the ATL and
SA/ RD/ AC, whichever is applicable, on a quarterly basis. This shall
help in reducing the balances of unliquidated cash advances
presented in the books;
b. Exert all possible efforts to reconcile the balances presented in the
books and schedule of cash advances to produce a more accurate
presentation of balances; and
c. For Trust Fund unliquidated accounts, confirm with the respective
source agencies whether the City Government still have outstanding
unliquidated fund transfers. If found to be liquidated, adjust the
books accordingly.

3.11 We reiterated our prior years audit recommendation that the
Management:
a. Require the coordination between CAO and CGSO towards the
implementation of the perpetual inventory method in the recording
of supplies and materials, to ensure proper accounting and promote
better control in the receipt and issuance of supplies and materials;
b. Effect necessary adjustments in the inventory account most
especially if the items no longer exist;
c. Instruct the CGSO and CAO to maintain updated Stock Cards and
SLC and regularly reconcile records to detect discrepancies and
effect immediate corrections.

4.8 We reiterated our prior years audit recommendation to provide for
Allowance for Impairment Accounts in the books in order to present
fairly the net realizable value of the Receivable Accounts in the
financial statements.

5.5 We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations that the LCE:
a. Direct the CAO to maintain and prepare PPELCs with the required
format as shown in Annex 9 on the MNGAS for LGUs; and
b. Direct the Property Supply Officer to ensure that the Property
Custodian keeps and maintains updated PC for each PPE account
to readily provide pertinent data, thus facilitate the preparation of
the Schedule of Depreciation by the City Accountant which shall be
the basis of recording in the books of accounts.

6.7 We recommended that the LCE instruct the City Accountant to:
a. properly investigate the reason behind non-reconciliation of
balances presented in the SCBAA and SFPer with the NFS; and
b. effect proper adjustments

7.6 We reiterated our prior year audit recommendation that the LCE
direct the City Accountant to faithfully observe the rules and
regulations on the preparation of FS by revisiting the provisions of the
IPSAS and other COA issuances particularly on the preparation and
presentation of accounts. For LGUs, COA Circular 2016-004 dated
September 30, 2016 and its annexes shall be used as a guidance for the
preparation and disclosures of FS.

8.7 We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to
undertake a detailed reconciliation/analysis of the accounts and
investigate causes of the error/discrepancies for the purpose of booking
up the necessary adjustments to arrive at the correct balances of the
negative accounts.

9.10 We recommended that the LCE instruct:
a. The City Treasurer to observe the guidelines prescribed under COA
Circular No. 2012-002 particularly the transfer of unspent
allotment of Current Year LDRMM Fund to its bank account at
year-end;
b. The City Accountant to carefully monitor the balances per ledgers,
schedules and reports and reconcile the balance of the Trust
Liability-DRRM with the Cash-in Bank; and
c. City Budget Officer to coordinate with the Local Finance
Committee in the monitoring and reconciliation of LDRRM budget,
cash availability and financial statement account balances to
determine amount still not fully utilized after five (5) years that
should be reverted back to the general fund.

10.4 We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to refer to
the COA chart of accounts when recording and classifying transactions
as to its appropriate accounts. Moreover, the Audit Team is always open
for consultation as to the proper recording of transactions.

11.11 We recommended that Local Finance Committee identify additional
sources of income and/or push for the passage of updated revenue code
to boost local taxation and design a systematic and realistic forecasting
of revenue collection based on the current state of the City
Government’s economic enterprises; and the City Treasurer to
monitor, intensify and strategize collection efforts.

12.7 We recommend that the LCE instruct the Acting City Treasurer to:
a. Properly orient the accountable officers/ special disbursing officers/
collectors before they assume their responsibilities especially on the
proper posting or recording, referencing and footing of the cash
book;
b. Properly inform and brief the concerned accountable officers in
advance regarding the conduct of cash examination; and
c. Always make the books, accounts, papers and cash, available at any
time during the conduct of audit.

13.7 We recommend that the Acting City Treasurer direct the Accountable
Form Custodian to:
a. Observe the provisions of PD 1445 and MNGAS for LGUs on the
recording and verification of accountable forms as well as
inspection of items received by the LGU;
b. If funds will allow, provide more space for warehousing so that the
forms may be stored in an organized manner; and
c. Always update the records of accountable forms.

14.5 We recommended that the LCE through the City Accountant to:
a. Investigate reasons of non-reconciliation of Inter-Agency
accounts; and
b. Prepare necessary adjusting entries to properly reflect the Inter-
Agency accounts based on the amount withheld on the month of
December, which is payable on January 2023.

15.8 We recommended that the:
a. City Health Officer require the unit price and total cost of all
received grants and donations upon receiving the items;
b. City Health Officer furnish the Accountant all necessary documents
regarding the received grants and donations in kind; and
c. City Accountant recognize in the books of accounts all received
grants and donations in kind in compliance with Paragraphs 96 and
97 of IPSAS No. 23.

16.10 We recommended that the LCE:
a. Instruct the City Accountant that before effecting payments of
claims, disbursement vouchers should be adequately supported
with the required documents. This is to ensure that the paid wages
were actually rendered in accordance with their assigned tasks and
their services were truly necessary.
b. Concerned job order workers be required to submit their DTRs and
individual accomplishment reports duly verified and signed by their
immediate supervisors in adherence to Section 4(6) of PD 1445 and
Section 1.2.1 of COA Circular 2012-001.

17.5 We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations that the non-
existing or missing PPE items without available record of
accountability be identified and be derecognized from the books of
accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-
time cleansing of PPE accounts of government agencies to establish
PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and
accountability.

We recommended that City Accountant apply the perpetual inventory method and the accounting standards of recording purchases of drugs, medicines, and relief goods (welfare goods) as Inventories, recognize the corresponding expenses for actual issuances to the units, and maintain SLC. On the other hand, the General Services Officer maintain Stock Cards and regularly reconcile with the ledger cards of the accounting unit.

We reiterated our previous years’ recommendation that the City Accountant require all the depository banks to strictly comply with the submission of the mandated bank statements (BS) and supporting documents for all depository accounts maintained by the City Government within five days after the end of each month. In turn, within ten days from the receipt of such BS, the same should be reconciled with the General Ledgers which shall be the basis for preparation of BRS; and henceforth, the BRS must be regularly submitted to the Auditor within ten days after the end of each month together with the paid checks and debit/credit memos. Provide for Allowance for Impairment Accounts in the books in order to present fairly the net realizable value of the Receivable Accounts in the financial statements.

observations.managementAction: Management averred that the services of the Consultants have proven to be vital and integral to the expert and proficient operation particularly the Office of the Governor, hence, they strongly requested for a reconsideration and undertake that in their subsequent hiring will abide by the procedure provided by law.                           At the onset, we wish to inform this Honorable Office that staring July 2021, in compliance with your previous AOM docketed as AOM No. 2021-13-17 (2020) dated March 04,2021, individuals performing services under the Governor’s Office are now being engaged under a Contract of Service (COS), and is thus exempt from the bidding requirements under Republic Act No. 9184. We have likewise previously filed a Comment to your earlier AOM No. 2021-13-17 (2020) last March 25,2021 which essentially contains the same finding/s as the present AOM.                           This notwithstanding, and in compliance with your present AOM, we are hereby respectfully submitting this Comment for your kind consideration.                           As explained in COA-DBM Joint Circular No. 2, series of 2020, government agencies such as Local Government Units (LGUs) are authorized to enter into service contracts with other individuals for services related or incident to their respective functions and operations, whether on a part-time or full-time basis.                           More particularly, Section 6.2 of the abovementioned circular explains that government agencies may enter into COS with individuals as consultants/ contractors, subject to the following guidelines:                           6.2.1 The term of contract between the agency and the individual contractor shall be for a maximum period of one (1) year, renewable at the option of the Head of the procuring entity, but in no case shall exceed the term of the latter.                           6.2.2 Engaging the service of an individual contractor shall be subject to pertinent provisions of R.A No. 9184 and its implementing guidelines, as applicable, and the existing budgeting, accounting, and auditing rules and regulations.                           To clarify the issue on whether R.A No. 9184 and its Revised Implementing Rules and Regulations apply in the engagement of individuals under a Job Order or Contract of Service, Government Procurement Policy Board (GPPB) Issued GPPB Policy Opinion 2012-11-21.                           In the said Policy Opinion, GPPB defined and distinguished (1) individuals engaged to perform Personal Services and (2) individuals engaged to perform Non- Personal Services under a Job order or Contract of Service. In the former (Personal Service), GPPB explained that the ensuing arrangement partakes the nature of hiring personnel rather than the procurement of services, and is thus not covered by R.A No. 9184. In the latter (Non-Personal Services), the employer-employee linkage is with the contracting firm and the individual workers because the power of selection, power of control, power to dismiss, and payment of wages rest in the hands of the firm or company with whom the procuring entity contracted with. Under this situation, R.A No. 9184 and its IRR apply in the procurement of the on-personal services provided by the firm or company.                           Moreover, it was likewise mentioned in the same Policy Opinion that the engagement of Personal Services under a Job Order or Contract of Service scenario is not contemplated under the procurement law and its associated rules. If at all, the rules on the procurement of General Support Services under R.A 9184 and its IRR applies to the engagement of an individual or company that will provide non-personal service, and not those in the nature of a personal service. The same opinion was once again echoed by the GPPB in its Policy Opinion No. 2014-05-05 wherein it was stated that “RA 9184 and its IRR do not apply in the engagement of services of individuals under Job Orde or Contract of Service; however, they apply in the engagement of non-personal service through sole proprietorship, partnership or a corporation contracted for the purpose.”                           In our case, the individuals that were hired under a Contract of Service are performing task that are essential to the conduct of business and affairs of the Governor’s Office as they are contracted to provide advisory support on various projects and programs of the Provincial Government of Isabela⎼ both ongoing and future. Therefore, based on the pronouncements made in GPPB Policy Opinion 2012-11-21, the tasks performed by these individuals partake the nature of Personal Service, and is thus exempt from the requirements R.A No. 9184.                           Submission of Accounts                           The covering disbursement vouchers (DVs) / journal entry vouchers (JEVs) of six (6) recorded transactions in the aggregate amount of P23,054,643.26 for the month of December 2021 remained unsubmitted as of date which precluded the audit team from conducting timely audit of financial transactions involved, rendering the balances of affected accounts unreliable.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to
strictly observe the rules and regulations on the preparation of the financial statements.
Also. the City Accountant to revisit the provisions of the International Public Sector
Accounting Standards (IPSAS) and other COA issuances particularly on the
preparation and presentation of restated financial statements. Moving forward to the
implementation of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, future restatements
are to be in accordance with Sections 343 to 345.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the City Accountant
to reconcile the difference between the actual amounts presented in the SCBAA and
the amount in the Statement of Financial Performance. Moving forward to the issuance
of the Government Accounting Manual for LGUs, to strictly follow the provisions for
SCBAA as described in sections 370 to 373 of Volume I.

We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations that the LCE directs the
City Accountant to faithfully observe the rules and regulations on the preparation of
the FS; to revisit the provisions of the IPSAS and other COA issuances particularly on
the preparation and presentation of accounts in the FS; and refer to COA Circular 2016-
004 dated September 30, 2016 and its annexes for the preparation and disclosures in
FS of Local Government Units.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the City Accountant to
undertake a detailed reconciliation/analysis of the accounts and investigate causes of
the error/discrepancies for the purpose of booking up the necessary adjustments to
arrive at the correct balances of the negative accounts.

We reiterated our previous years’ audit recommendation that the City
Accountant require all the depository banks to strictly comply with the submission of
the mandated bank statements (BS) and supporting documents for all depository
accounts maintained by the City Government within five days after the end of each
month. In turn, within ten days from the receipt of such BS, the same should be
reconciled with the General Ledgers which shall be the basis for preparation of BRS;
and henceforth, the BRS must be regularly submitted to the Auditor within ten days
after the end of each month together with the paid checks and debit/credit memos.
Likewise, prepare the necessary journal entry vouchers (JEVs) to record the reconciling
items affecting the accounts.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the Management direct the
City Accountant to reconcile with the City Treasurer the Cash Accounts records on a
quarterly basis to reflect an accurate and reliable balance at any particular period.

We reiterated the following recommendations:

13

Make sure that a cash advance shall be reported on and liquidated as soon as
the purpose for which it was given has been served, or within the periods
required for its settlement;

Closely monitor the submission of the liquidation reports by sending:

a. Written reminders (demand letters) to the officers and employees
concerned enjoining them to liquidate their cash advances within the
prescribed periods, or as soon as the purpose for which these were given
have been served, and demand letters in case of failure to comply with
the written reminders.

b. Send tracers to follow-up demand letters sent.

Apply requests for write-off of dormant account to the ATL and SA/ RD/ AC,
whichever is applicable, on a quarterly basis. This shall help in reducing the
balances of unliquidated cash advances presented in the books.

Exert all possible efforts to reconcile the balances presented in the books and
schedule of cash advances to produce a more accurate presentation of balances.
For Trust Fund unliquidated accounts, exert and effort to confirm with the
respective source agencies whether they still have outstanding unliquidated
fund transfers.

We also recommend that the management should also consider instituting
sanctions against the officials or employees concerned such as withholding or
suspending payment of their salaries, or other appropriate legal sanction provided
for under the regulation.

We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations that the Local Chief Executive:
a. Direct the City Accounting Office to maintain and prepare PPELC with the
required format as shown in Annex 9 on the MNGAS for LGUs and update the
same.
b. The City Accountant and Other Officers concerned should provide for the
depreciation on the depreciable assets. The Property Supply Officer should
ensure that the Property Custodian keeps and maintains updated Property Card
for each PPE account to readily provide pertinent data, thus facilitate the
preparation of the Schedule of Depreciation which shall be the basis of
recording in the books of accounts the appropriate depreciation expenses
pertaining to the current year which should be charged against its appropriate
depreciation expense accounts. These are necessary in order to present fairly
the PPE accounts with its corresponding Accumulated Depreciation Expense
Accounts in the FS.

We reiterated our prior years audit recommendation that the management:

(1) Require the coordination between the City Accountant and the GSO towards the
implementation of the perpetual inventory method in the recording of supplies and
materials to ensure proper accounting and promote better control in the receipt and
issuance of supplies and materials;

(2) Determine causes of non-recording of drugs and medicines in the inventory and its
related expense account;

(3) Make proper adjustments in the inventory accounts most especially if the items do
no longer exist. Inventories already issued must be expensed already:

(4) Instruct the GSO and the City Accountant to maintain updated Stock Cards and
SLC and regularly reconcile records to detect discrepancies and effect immediate
corrections.
(5) Cstablish relevant inventory account vited above.

We reiterated our prior years audit recommendation to provide for Allowance
for Impairment Accounts in the books in order to present fairly the net realizable
value of the Receivable Accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Agriculturist be directed to conduct the physical
inventory of the Biological Asset. The City Accountant must also recognize the same
in the books. Moving forward to the implementation of the Government Accounting
Manual for LGUs, Biological Assets transactions are to be in accordance with Sections
304 to 315.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to instruct:

a. The City Treasurer to observe the guidelines prescribed under COA
Circular No. 2012-002 particularly the transfer of unspent allotment of
Current Year LDRMM Fund to its bank account at year-end;

b. The City Accountant to carefully monitor the balances per ledgers,
schedules and reports and reconcile the balance of the Trust Liability-
DRRM with the Cash-in Bank; and

c. City Budget Officer to coordinate with the Local Finance Committee in the
monitoring and reconciliation of LDRRM budget, cash availability and
financial statement account balances to determine amount still not fully
utilized after five (5) years that should be reverted back to the general fund.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct:

1. The City Budget Officer to review the budget of the different
Departments/Office as to the correct expense classification and include on
Other MOE only those which cannot be classified under any specific
expense titles pursuant to COA Circular No 2015-009. In case the Other
MOE account is used, specify the items of expenditures to be charged to the
said account; and

2. The City Accountant to record all the transactions of the City using the
proper accounts prescribe in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs so
that transactions are faithfully and correctly presented in the financial statements
to provide reliable data to the users thereof.

We recommended that the City Accountant be directed to properly review the
COA chart of accounts and properly classify future transactions as to their proper
accounts. Further, the Office of the Team Leader is always open for consultation as to
the proper recording of unique transactions.

We recommended that Local Finance Committee to identify additional sources
of income and/or push for the passage of updated revenue code to boost local taxation
and design a systematic and realistic forecasting of revenue collection based on the
current state of the City Government’s economic enterprises; and the City Treasurer to
monitor, intensify and strategize collection efforts.

We recommended that:

1. The HRMO and Accounting Office submit the required documents as
enumerated above to ascertain the legality, validity and propriety of subject
transaction and that onwards, claims should be duly processed and
supported with complete documents before it should be paid, to avoid
suspensions and/or disallowance in audit.

2. The Accounting Office to stop the practice of processing transactions that
have incomplete documents.

We recommended that the City Assessor to regulatly conduct general revision
of assessment of real property every three (3) years in accordance with the existing
laws, rules and regulations.

We recommended that adherence to Section 5 of RA 636 to protects the City
Government against any damage or loss properties or assets and interests due to fire,
carthquake, storm, or other fortuitous events/casualty. Submit yearly the Property
Inventory Form to the Government Service Insurance System and to the Office of the
Supervising Auditor/Audit Team Leader every 30th day of April.

We reiterated our prior year audit recommendations that the non-existing or
missing PPE items without available record of accountability be identified and be
derecognized from the books of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No, 2020-
006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of government agencies to establish
PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We reiterated our prior year audit recommendation that the General Services
Office prepares the RLRN and RPCRNS with the Inventory Committee's help.
Furthermore, we recommend that the City Accountant maintain the subsidiary ledger
for roads and their components, reconcile it with the result of the physical count at year-
end, correctly depreciate the accounts, and disclose it in the NFS as prescribed.

We reiterated our prior year audit recommendation that the LCE:

a. Require the supply/property inspector to conduct pre-repair
inspection and post repair inspection to LGU properties that are subjected
to repair and furnish the reports to the COA Auditors within five (5) days from the
date of each of the inspection activity;

b. Direct the General Services Officer to maintain the records regarding the history
of repairs of each of the properties of the LGU; and

c. Direct the City Accountant not to process claims pertaining to repairs and
maintenance of motor vehicles and heavy equipment with lacking supporting
documents.

We reiterated our prior year audit recommendation that the Management:

a) Appropriate one percent (1%) of its Annual Budget for the Senior Citizen and
PWD and ensure optimal utilization of the fund to fully uphold the goal of
addressing the concerns of senior citizens and persons with disability; and,

b) Make sure that charges intended for Senior Citizens and Person with
Disabilities are made in the appropriate fund.

We recommended that the City Budget Officer be directed to comply with the provisions of the COA 2016-004 pertaining to the timely submission of appropriate registries and records to the City Accounting Office to facilitate preparation of the SCBAA. Also, we recommend both the Accounting and Budget Offices to reconcile their records regularly (monthly, if possible) so that any discrepancies with the records, if cannot be avoided, detected early and the necessary and timely review and examination can be made immediately.

We recommended that the non-existing or missing PPE items without available record of accountability be identified and be derecognized from the books of accounts in accordance with COA Circular No. 2020-006 for the one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of government agencies to establish PPE balances that are verifiable as to existence, condition and accountability.

We recommended that the management inform the COA office for the deliveries of procured equipment, goods, materials and equipment for proper inspection of the items within 24 hours as provided under COA Circular No. 2009-002.

We recommended that a copy of contract or PO, irrespective of amount, and together with each and every supporting documents, be submitted to the auditor within 5 days upon issuance thereof for the auditorial, legal and technical review.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct:

(a) the appropriate collecting officer of the LGU, not the BAC, to receive the collection arising from sale of bidding docs, via an Official Receipt and properly transmit the same to the City Treasurer’s Office.

(b) the City Treasurer to record the collections in the RCD and deposit such collections intact in the bank.

(c) the City Accountant to make the necessary Journal Entry to record the said collection and post the same to the Ledger.
We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant and the City Treasurer to submit all the required monthly financial reports necessary in the audit on or before the due date.

We recommended that the management discontinue accepting collection receipts in place of the sales invoice or official receipts as proof of payment. On the other hand, we recommend that the City Treasurer demand from the supplier for the sales invoices or official receipts to obtain principal evidence on the payment of goods.

We recommended that the LCE direct the City Treasurer through the City Accountant to submit to the auditor the 97 cancelled checks for validation and henceforth, ensure that all cancelled checks are attached to the RCI and reported chronologically with the other checks issued with the word “Cancelled” clearly indicated therein.
We recommended that the City Treasurer immediately stamp PAID all DVs and its relating supporting documents after the payment had been made to prevent possible reuse of the documents.

We recommended that the LCE:

a. Require the supply/property inspector to conduct pre-repair inspection and post repair inspection to LGU properties that are subjected to repair and furnish the reports to the COA Auditors within five (5) days from the date of each of the inspection activity;

b. Direct the General Services Officer to maintain the records regarding the history of repairs of each of the properties of the LGU; and

c. Direct the City Accountant not to process claims pertaining to repairs and maintenance of motor vehicles and heavy equipment with lacking supporting documents.
We recommended that the LCE direct the City Accountant to revisit the provisions of the IPSAS 42 and to properly disclose the needed disclosures in the ES.
We recommended that the LGU, particularly, the CSWD comply with the pertinent requirements and regulations concerning the ESP and SAP and submit the lacking documents to the audit team.

We recommended that the City comply with the Requirements prescribed under LBC 125 and submit the monthly report using the prescribed format (Annex C) to the auditor’s office. Moreover, (a) direct the City Budget Officer in coordination with the City Accountant to prepare the required reports on a monthly basis starting from the receipt of the BGCM until the same will be fully disbursed and furnish copies to the Public Information Officer and Information and Communication Technology Officer; and (b) instruct the latter two officers to post the reports in at least three conspicuous places and in the official website under the transparency seal, respectively and immediately inform the LCE of such posting so that the latter can send a written notice to the appropriate government agencies that said reports have been posted in the official website of the LGU.

We recommended that management, specifically the BAC, observe the GPPB Circular No. 01-2020, GPPB Advisory No. 05-2020, GPPB NPM No. 004-2020 and RIRR of RA No. 9184 in the emergency procurement under the Bayanihan Act; and submit the lacking documents to the Audit Team. Moreover, the City Accountant examine the completeness of the supporting documents for each disbursement to ensure compliance with the basic and mandatory documentary requirements.

We recommended that the Management:

a) instruct the Accounting Processor that before effecting payments of claims, disbursement vouchers should be adequately supported with the required documents; and

b) comply strictly with Section 4 of PD No. 1445 and COA Circular No. 2012-001.

We recommended that management prepare and submit its CY 2019 Agency Action Plan and Status of Implementation - (AAPSI) within the period prescribed by the law using the prescribed form under COA Memorandum No. 2014 — 002 dated March 18, 2014. It is further recommended that a focal person be designated by the LCE who will consolidate the report before submission to the audit team.

We reiterated our prior year recommendation that the Management direct the City Accountant to reconcile with the City Treasurer the Cash Accounts records on a quarterly basis to reflect an accurate and reliable balance at any particular period in compliance with Paragraph C, Section 181, Volume 1 of GAAM.

We reiterated the following recommendations:

1. Make sure that a cash advance shall be reported on and liquidated as soon as the purpose for which it was given has been served, or within the periods required for its settlement;

2. Closely monitor the submission of the liquidation reports by sending:

a. Written reminders (demand letters) to the officers and employees concerned enjoining them to liquidate their cash advances within the prescribed periods, or as soon as the purpose for which these were given have been served, and demand letters in case of failure to comply with the written reminders.

b. Send tracers to follow-up demand letters sent.

3. Apply requests for write-off of dormant account to the ATL and SA/ RD/ AC, whichever is applicable, on a quarterly basis. This shall help in reducing the balances of unliquidated cash advances presented in the books.

4. Exert all possible efforts to reconcile the balances presented in the books and schedule of cash advances to produce a more accurate presentation of balances.

We also recommend that the management should also consider instituting sanctions against the officials or employees concerned such as withholding or suspending payment of their salaries, or other appropriate legal sanction provided for under the regulation.

We recommended the Local Chief Executive to:

a. Direct the Inventory and Disposal Committee to conduct a complete physical count of PPE, annually, as required and to submit the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RCPPE) and Inventory and Inspection Report of Unserviceable Properties to the auditor concerned once completed. The CGSO shall furnish the CAO the electronic and hard copies of the RPCPPE for their reconciliation with the accounting records; and The CAO shall determine any discrepancies between the RPCPPE and accounting records and prepare the necessary Subsidiary Ledger to record all unaccounted PPE items. Upon determination of all the City’s unserviceable assets, the Accountant is tasked to reclassify it to Other Assets account or dropped it from the books once disposed, whichever is applicable.

b. Intensify efforts to maintain and prepare PPELC with the required format as shown in Annex 9 on the MNGAS for LGUs.

c. The City Accountant and Other Officers concerned should provide for the depreciation on the depreciable assets. The Property Supply Officer should ensure that the Property Custodian keeps and maintains updated Property Card for each PPE account to readily provide pertinent data, thus facilitate the preparation of the Schedule of Depreciation which shall be the basis of recording in the books of accounts the appropriate depreciation expenses pertaining to the current year which should be charged against its appropriate depreciation expense accounts. These are necessary in order to present fairly the PPE accounts with its corresponding Accumulated Depreciation Expense Accounts in the FS.

d. Moreover, require the heads of the CAO and CGSO to ensure the conduct of the required annual and semestral physical count of PPE and inventories, respectively, and timely reconciliation with the Accounting and Supply records against the related PPELC and Property Cards, and SLC and Stock Cards to facilitate timely verification and adjustment of any discrepancies noted.

e. Require the CAO to adhere to the prescribed accounting rules relative to the asset recognition under the Construction in Progress account and to provide necessary adjusting entries to reclassify the misclassified PPE accounts (CIP and specific PPE accounts).

We recommended that the City Mayor:

a) Require the GAD Focal Person to prepare on time the annual GPB for review and approval by DILG and submit the approved copy and the corresponding AR to the COA Audit Team within the prescribed period provided in COA Circular No. 2014-001 dated March 18, 2014; and

b) Approve charges and disbursements on GAD Fund only if supported with DILG reviewed and approved GPB to ascertain validity and propriety of disbursements.

c) In case of revised GPB, revisions should be made and effected prior to the passing of the annual budget, otherwise, realignment of expenditure items should be made. Any changes pertaining to the GPB and Budget should be informed to the Audit Team assigned.

We recommended and the Inventory Committee agreed to  complete  the
conduct of physical inventory taking, render a  report  thereon  and  update
and reconcile the related subsidiary records  to  present  fairly  the  PPEs
accounts at year-end.

         We also recommended that complete physical inventory reports  shall
be submitted to the  Auditor  not  later  than  January  31  of  each  year.
Henceforth, periodic inventory  taking  and  reconciliation  of  records  be
regularly undertaken and regarded with utmost importance.

We recommended and the Municipal  Accountant  and  Municipal  Treasurer
agreed to timely reconcile records per cashbook as well as the  general  and
subsidiary ledgers before the latter foot and close the books at the end  of
each month.


    We  also  recommended  that  the  two  offices  must  conduct   regular
reconciliation of records at  least  every  end  of  each  quarter  and  any
adjustment must be corrected and resolved promptly.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor thru  the  Municipal  Accountant
agreed to strictly require officials and  employees  who  have  unliquidated
cash advances to settle the amount advanced within  the  prescribed  period.
Otherwise, withhold their salaries until such time that  the  cash  advances
are fully settled.


    We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure  that
no additional cash advance shall be granted  to  any  official  or  employee
with pending unliquidated cash advance.

We recommended and the Bids and Awards  Committee  agreed  to  strictly
comply  with  the  existing  regulations  regarding  the  completeness   and
propriety  of  bidding  documents  to  establish  the  regularity   of   the
procurement process.



    We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to ensure  that
all the required and necessary supporting  documents  are  attached  to  the
claims  to  establish  the  validity,  legality   and   propriety   of   the
disbursements.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor  agreed  that  the  Municipality
will strengthen  its  information  dissemination  and  COVID-19  vaccination
campaign to fight vaccine hesitancy and achieve the herd immunity.

We recommended and the LVOC Nurse Coordinator  agreed  to  furnish  the
Municipal Accountant with the detailed list of allocated  vaccines  received
by  the  Municipality.  Furthermore,  we  recommended  that  the   Municipal
Accountant recognize in the books all the donations received  in  accordance
with existing regulations.


    In case of donations received without  cost,  we  recommended  and  the
Municipal Accountant agreed to recognize the donations at their fair  market
value as at the date of receipt, which may be ascertained  by  reference  to
an active market or by appraisal as provided in paragraph 97  of  the  IPSAS
NO. 23.

We recommended and the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  ensure  that
expenses/obligations are taken up in the book of accounts in  the  year  the
said expenses were incurred and  delivered,  otherwise  record  as  accounts
payable at year-end.

We recommended and the MSWD Officer agreed to  ensure  the  preparation
and submission of the Summary/List of Donations  Received,  Distributed  and
Balances to appropriate offices, copy furnish the COA  Auditor,  within  ten
(10) days  after  the  end  of  the  quarter  to  enable  proper  recording,
monitoring and evaluation of donations received by the Municipality.

We recommended and the Municipal Mayor agreed to direct  the  Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal DRRM Officer to coordinate with each other  for
the preparation and submission of the Report on Sources and  Utilization  of
LDRRMF to the Office of the Auditor on or before the 15th day after the  end
of each month.

We recommended and the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  require  the
Municipal Health Officer  to  submit  immediately  the  required  supporting
documents to establish the validity of SRA payment.


    We also recommended and  the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  ensure
strict  compliance  with  the  prescribed  guidelines  before  granting  any
allowances to personnel.

We recommended and the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  require  the
Municipal Health Officer  to  submit  immediately  the  required  supporting
documents to establish the validity of AHDP payment.


    We also recommended and  the  Municipal  Accountant  agreed  to  ensure
strict  compliance  with  the  prescribed  guidelines  before  granting  any
allowances to personnel.

We recommended that the LGUs who received these prefabricated building materials be required to immediately implement the project, as it may still be used for other public purposes, and the concerned personnel may be held liable if materials are no longer complete or destroyed or have deteriorated while warehoused or stockpiled.
Further, we recommended close monitoring until the completion of all provincial projects to ensure that the good intention of the Provincial Government of Isabela in using public funds could be achieved at optimum.

We recommended that Management thru the Office of the Provincial Treasurer, observe the following measures in the release of financial assistance to eligible client-beneficiary/ies, to wit:
Instruct the claimant to sign the portion of the Payroll corresponding to his/her name in accordance with the signature as appearing in his/her submitted Identification Card/Certificate of Eligibility/General Intake Sheet; and
Require the claimant-beneficiary to submit a photocopy of his/her valid ID and if practicable, to authenticate the same by affixing his/her signature thereon.
We likewise recommended that Management thru the Offices of the Provincial Treasurer and Provincial Accountant, direct the submission of the lacking documents pertaining to the afore-listed client/beneficiaries.
We further recommended that Management thru the Office of the Provincial Accountant, direct the submission by the concerned PGI-PESO Manager of the certified list of clients/beneficiaries of I-RISE financial assistance to individuals/families (Tricycle Operators and Drivers' Association-TODA) at the Municipality of Cabagan, Isabela, and to furnish us copy of the Memorandum of Agreement, if any, between the DOF- Bureau of the Treasury and PGI.

We recommended that Management thru the Office of the Provincial Accountant, require the implementing LGUs to submit report on the utilization of the funds transferred to them which remained long outstanding or to cause the return of the said funds in case where the programmed project was not implemented.

We recommended that Management, thru the Provincial Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Office, maximize the utilization of DRRM funds and strictly monitor the implementation of planned programs/projects/activities to ensure the delivery of services to the public and benefits therefrom are fully achieved.

We recommended that the Provincial Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer (PDRRMO) include the unexpended balance of the previous years in the annual preparation of its Local Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Fund Investment Plan (LDRRMFIP)/Annual Investment Plan (AIP) to properly monitor the implementation and use of the fund.

We recommended that Management, thru the Office of the Provincial Accountant, strictly follow the guidelines and procedures of COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016 on the write-off of dormant receivable accounts, unliquidated cash advances, and fund transfers which shall cause the fair presentation of the receivable accounts in the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Management carefully assess the collectability of its loans receivable and provide an allowance for impairment on its non-moving accounts to fairly present the balance in the Financial Statement.
We further recommended that Subsidiary Ledgers be updated with the necessary information of accounts such as the address of the borrower, contact numbers and the breakdown of the outstanding balance.

We recommended that Management strictly adhere with the timely submission of report on the physical count of inventories; Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE); and Biological Asset to support the balances of accounts presented in its Financial Statement.
Additionally, we recommended that Management comply with the prescribed guidelines and procedures under COA Circular No. 2020-006 dated January 31, 2020 in the conduct of physical count of PPE; recognition of PPE items found at the station; and disposition for non-existing/missing PPE items for the one-time cleansing of PPE account balances.
We also reminded the inventory team to invite a representative from the Audit Team as a witness during the inventory taking.

We recommended that Management:
pursue measures that would protect the interest of the Provincial Government of Isabela (PGI) and its constituents from investment in idle funds, and to invest much needed resources in other projects, programs, and activities that will provide immediate benefit and would address the most urgent needs of the public; and
apprise this Office on the status of any plan of actions by the Board of Directors of the Northeastern Luzon Pacific Coastal Service, Inc. (NLPCSI) for the CY 2021, for our evaluation and appreciation in audit. Likewise, we request an authenticated copy of the Shares of Stock of the NLPCSI held by the PGI, for our Office file.

We recommended the OIC-Provincial Accountant to require from the Provincial General Services Office (PGSO)/Disposal Committee the properly accomplished Inventory and Inspection Report of the Unserviceable Property (IIRUP) together with a copy of the Official Receipt (if the disposal is for value received) to ensure that there is proper disposal of supplies and properties that became unserviceable or are no longer needed before dropping the book value of the subject property from the books of accounts.
Likewise, we required from the PGSO/Disposal Committee the submission of additional supporting documents evidencing the actual disposal made.

We recommended the Management, thru the Office of the Provincial Accountant and in coordination with the Provincial Budget Officer, to ensure that liabilities recorded in the books of the Province are duly supported with complete and necessary documents to support the existence and validity of the obligations.

We recommended that Management direct the concerned personnel to submit authenticated photocopies of the lacking documents enumerated above in support of the disbursement vouchers on the payments of the contract as required under COA Circular No. 2012-001.  If for any reason the requested documents could not be produced, the submission of written justification is further recommended so that we may properly evaluate the reason for non-compliance thereof.

We recommended that Management, thru the Office of the Provincial Treasurer, to observe the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units (MNGAS-LGUs) guideline in the release of check to the payee or to his authorized representative, and in case where the claimant is not the payee or the payee’s purported representative, to require the presentation of the document evidencing his authority to collect/claim the check in behalf of the payee.

We recommended Management thru the Assistant Provincial Accountant to submit pertinent documents showing compliance with the procedure provided by law in hiring consultants for CY 2021, for evaluation by our Team to determine the necessary audit action to be performed.

We recommended the immediate submission of six (6) journal entry vouchers and its corresponding approved disbursement vouchers and supporting papers in the aggregate amount of P23,054,643.26 in order for us to determine the validity and propriety of said transactions.
Further, we recommended that the Office of the Provincial Accountant should ensure that all accounts and supporting papers that are included in the transmittal reports must be complete at the time of submission to avoid further delay in the conduct of audit. A representative from the Accounting Office should be present during inventory of submitted documents to counter check the result of the inventory.

observations.recommendation: Reconcile the Cashier-Designee’s book balance with the accounting records at least once in every quarter in accordance with accounting and auditing regulations; to serve as an effective control measure to avoid errors in the reporting and recording of collections and deposits;                           Require the Liquidating Officer (LO), before preparing his Report of Collection and Deposit (RCD), to conduct a thorough verification of the RCDs of the Cashier-Designate to ascertain the accuracy of the remitted/deposited collections vis-à-vis the amounts indicated in the Official Receipts;                           Ascertain the accuracy of the RCDs of all AOs/LOs before preparing the RCD of the Provincial Treasurer;                           Require the Provincial Treasurer/Cashier or any authorized employee under her office other than the cashier-designate or all other field office AOs/LOs, to sign the deposit slips and actually make the deposits in accordance with Sections 32 and 35 of the MNGAS-LGUs;                           Require the Cashier-Designate to:                           Reconcile on a daily frequency the cashbook balance with that of the cash on hand to avoid the recurrence of errors;                           Exercise the utmost care in the recording of transactions in the cashbook, as basis for the accurate reporting of collections in the RCD; and                           Ensure that RCDs are prepared together with the turnover of all collections at the close of each business day;                           Require a written explanation from the Cashier-Designate within seventy-two (72) hours from receipt of the letter requiring the same for:                           the various errors in recording and reporting collections and deposits during the period under cash examination;                           the deviation from the prescribed requirement of RCD preparation at the close of each business day; and                           the departure from the prescribed turnover of collections at the close of each business day resulting to lengthy delays in the remittance of collections and maintaining huge accumulated daily balances of the collections after each remittance/deposit while making partial deposits instead of depositing the full amount of collections on hand at the end of each business day.
observations.managementAction: Management commented that the AO will abide by the guidelines in the handling, custody and recording of cash accountability. They averred that the errors found on the Report of Collections and Deposits have already been verified and corrected upon AO’s submission of reports to the Provincial Treasurer's Office.
observations.reason: Result of validation showed there were still discrepancies found in the accounts of AO. Reiterated under Finding No. 8
